# Checklist — Elicitation Activity (BABOK v3, Chapter 4)

> Reference checklist derived from **BABOK® Guide v3, Chapter 4 — Elicitation and Collaboration** with cross-references to Chapters 2 (Key Concepts), 3.2 (Plan Stakeholder Engagement), 9 (Underlying Competencies), and 10 (Techniques).
> Each row defines one verification check that should be answered **before, during, or right after** an elicitation activity. The checklist is the validation gate of the `babok-v3-software-requirements` skill.
> **Ordered by severity of a *missing* check** (Critical → High → Medium → Low). Severity scale:
>
> - **Critical** — absence directly leads to elicitation failure (wrong / missing requirements, broken trust, late discovery of stakeholders).
> - **High** — absence likely produces ambiguity, conflict, rework.
> - **Medium** — absence reduces effectiveness or auditability.
> - **Low** — hygiene gap with limited near-term impact.

## Checklist

| ID | Check name | Reference (§) | Severity | What must be verified | Concrete example (✅ success / ❌ failure) |
|----|------------|---------------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| EL-001 | Elicitation goal is explicit and shared | §4.1.4 .1; §10.25.3 .1 | Critical | A clear, specific, written goal exists for the activity (and, for an interview, for each interviewee), and was communicated **before** the session. | ✅ "Goal of the session: capture business rules for refund eligibility (REQ family REF-*) — pre-shared with the SME." ❌ Meeting starts with "let's talk about refunds" — drifts for 60 min with no usable output. |
| EL-002 | Stakeholder analysis performed; all relevant classes identified | §3.2.4 .1; §10.43 | Critical | All stakeholder classes (BABOK generic roles) have been identified; none is missing; roles, attitudes, decision authority, and influence are mapped (stakeholder list + matrix / onion / RACI). | ✅ Stakeholder map shows Sponsor, Customer, Domain SMEs, End Users, Implementation SMEs, Ops, PM, Regulator (GDPR), Supplier (payments), Tester — each with attitude, authority, influence. ❌ Only the PM and one product owner consulted; regulator and ops surface 6 weeks before go-live with showstoppers. |
| EL-003 | Right technique(s) selected for the situation | §4.1.4 .2; §10.25–§10.50 | Critical | At least two techniques are combined; chosen against cost, time, source access, culture, stakeholder availability and location, desired output. Not "always interviews". | ✅ Mix: 6 SME interviews + 1 workshop + observation of 2 call-center shifts + GDPR document analysis. ❌ Single questionnaire sent to 200 people, 18 % response, treated as complete input. |
| EL-004 | Logistics planned (participants, roles, time, place, channel, language, agenda) | §4.1.4 .3 | Critical | Goal, participants and roles, scheduled people / rooms / tools, location, communication channel, technique, languages (oral and written), and agenda are set before the activity. | ✅ Calendar invite includes agenda + objectives + pre-read + role of each attendee; remote attendees confirmed; language confirmed. ❌ Half the attendees join without context; half can't access the prototype; the activity wastes 20 min on setup. |
| EL-005 | Stakeholders prepared (buy-in and pre-read) | §4.1.4 .5 | Critical | Stakeholders received the agenda and supporting material in advance, understand the technique, and have given buy-in. Education / explanation provided to non-participants who will consume the output. | ✅ Pre-read sent 48 h before; SMEs confirmed availability and reviewed material; sponsor authorised participation. ❌ SMEs arrive cold; refuse to engage because they "have no idea why they're there". |
| EL-006 | Results captured during the session | §4.2.4 .2 | Critical | Decisions, ideas, open items, conflicts and follow-ups are visibly recorded **during** the activity (notes, board, scribe), not reconstructed later from memory. | ✅ Scribe captures live in a shared doc; closed by the facilitator at end-of-session. ❌ "I'll write it up later" → 30 % of the content is lost or distorted within 24 h. |
| EL-007 | Results confirmed with source / interviewee within a short window | §4.3.4 .1; §10.25.3 .6 | Critical | A confirmation pass closes the loop with the interviewee / source — **as soon as possible** after the activity — so they can correct or complete what was captured. | ✅ Notes shared within 24 h; corrections received within 72 h; signed off in the shared doc. ❌ Notes sent 3 weeks later; SME no longer remembers details; corrections are wrong. |
| EL-008 | Cross-source consistency verified | §4.3.4 .2 | Critical | Results from this activity are cross-checked against results of other elicitation activities; contradictions surfaced and escalated to Item Tracking. | ✅ Diff against last week's workshop on the same topic flags one contradictory rule; Item ITEM-014 opened, owner assigned. ❌ Two requirements documents say opposite things about the same rule; both shipped to dev. |
| EL-009 | Disambiguation rules applied for vague language | §3.1 (cf. SWEBOK §3.1); §10.25.3 .3 | Critical | The interviewer challenged every vague modifier ("fast", "user-friendly", "secure", "scalable", "always available", "reliable", "compliant") with a measurable follow-up; documented the answer. | ✅ "fast" → "p95 ≤ 800 ms at 1k RPS"; "secure" → "RBAC, MFA for admin, audit log, GDPR Art. 32". ❌ Spec ships with "the system must be fast and secure" verbatim. |
| EL-010 | 5-Whys applied when stakeholder proposes a solution | §2.5; §9.1.4 | Critical | When the stakeholder states a *solution* ("add an Excel export button"), the interviewer repeated "why?" until reaching the underlying need ("monthly reconciliation"). | ✅ Captured: need = "month-end reconciliation"; possible solutions = export, dashboard, automated report; choice deferred to design. ❌ Team builds the Excel export; users still spend 3 days/month reconciling manually. |
| EL-011 | All four BABOK requirement types are addressed | §2.3 | High | The activity has addressed Business / Stakeholder / Solution (Functional + Non-Functional) / Transition requirements, not only one or two. | ✅ Notes are sectioned: Business goals (REV-*), Stakeholder needs (US-*), Functional FR-*, NFR-*, Transition (data migration, training, cutover). ❌ Only FR captured; transition planning surfaces a 2-week data migration risk one week before go-live. |
| EL-012 | All six BACCM concepts considered | §2.1 | High | Change / Need / Solution / Stakeholder / Value / Context have each been asked about; a missing concept signals an incomplete analysis. | ✅ Coverage matrix shows each concept addressed by at least one captured statement. ❌ The session focused on "Solution" and "Change" — never asked about "Value" or "Context"; team builds something users won't adopt. |
| EL-013 | Open and closed questions used appropriately | §10.25.3 .3 | High | Open questions used to elicit new information / dialogue; closed questions used to confirm or quantify. Mix is intentional, not random. | ✅ Interview guide alternates "describe how you currently …" with "is it 1, 2 or 3 per day?". ❌ All yes/no questions — interview produces a checklist of confirmations, no insight; or all open — interview drifts and never converges. |
| EL-014 | Leading questions avoided | §10.25.4 .2 | High | The interviewer did not embed an answer in the question; phrased neutrally to avoid biasing the interviewee. | ✅ "How do you handle exceptions?" ❌ "You agree that the current process is too slow, right?" |
| EL-015 | Non-verbal cues observed and acted on | §9.4.2; §10.25.3 .5 | High | The interviewer monitored body language, tone, hesitation; when verbal and non-verbal cues disagreed, explored further instead of accepting the words. | ✅ SME says "we're fine with that" while shaking head → BA pauses and probes; surfaces a real objection. ❌ Words taken at face value; resistance only surfaces at UAT. |
| EL-016 | Active listening practised | §9.4.4; §10.25.3 .5 | High | The interviewer summarised, paraphrased, and reflected back what the interviewee said before moving on; gave undivided attention; deferred judgment. | ✅ "Let me re-state to confirm: …" — corrected by the SME on the spot. ❌ Interviewer types while the SME talks; misses 40 % of what was said. |
| EL-017 | Decision-making authority confirmed before pursuing decisions | §3.2.4 .1 (Decision Making Authority); §9.5.4 | High | The interviewer confirmed who can approve / reject decisions in scope; collaborated with the right authority instead of with whoever was available. | ✅ Decision on refund window negotiated with both finance and legal owners. ❌ Decision taken with a junior PM; reversed by legal one month later. |
| EL-018 | Conflicting stakeholder positions surfaced and tracked | §4.5.4 .1; §9.5.4; §10.26 | High | Conflicts between stakeholders are recorded in Item Tracking with owners and a resolution path (negotiation / product family / escalation), not silently picked by the BA. | ✅ Item ITEM-021 "currency unit conflict EUR vs USD" tracked to resolution with both stakeholder reps. ❌ BA picks EUR silently; US customer rejects UAT. |
| EL-019 | Acceptance criteria captured for each solution / stakeholder requirement | §10.1; §10.48.3 .4 | High | Each captured requirement has measurable, testable acceptance criteria, ideally as ATDD / BDD scenarios. | ✅ Each user story has Given/When/Then scenarios with concrete inputs and expected outputs. ❌ "User can search products" — no criteria; dev guesses; QA can't validate. |
| EL-020 | Non-functional requirements categorised and quantified | §10.30 | High | NFRs are placed in the BABOK categories (availability, compatibility, functionality, maintainability, performance efficiency, portability, reliability, scalability, security, usability, certification, compliance, localization, SLAs, extensibility) and quantified with units, percentile, conditions. | ✅ "Login p95 ≤ 300 ms at 1k RPS sustained 10 min." ❌ "Must be fast and scalable." |
| EL-021 | Compliance / regulatory constraints captured as requirements | §10.30 (Compliance, Certification); §3.2 (Regulator stakeholder) | High | Applicable laws / regulations / standards (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, sector-specific) are listed; each obligation traces to one or more requirements. | ✅ Compliance matrix maps "GDPR Art. 32 → REQ-SEC-014..028". ❌ Compliance assumed; first DSAR (data subject access request) takes 6 weeks. |
| EL-022 | Domain vocabulary captured in glossary | §10.23 | High | Every domain term and acronym used in the captured information is added to a shared glossary with a single agreed definition; new terms reviewed at each session. | ✅ "Reconciliation = matching booked transactions to bank statements at end-of-day; owner: Finance." ❌ "Reconciliation" means three different things to three stakeholders; spec is interpreted differently by each team. |
| EL-023 | Open issues tracked in Item Tracking with owner and due date | §10.26; §4.5.4 .2 | High | Every issue / question / assumption / constraint / dependency raised during the session is logged with owner, due date, priority, status. | ✅ 14 items in the issue log after the session; each with owner and target date; reviewed weekly. ❌ Items in a personal notebook; lost; same questions re-asked 3 sessions later. |
| EL-024 | Stakeholder engagement monitored over time | §4.5.4 .2 | Medium | The BA monitors attendance, attitudes, timeliness of confirmations, kept commitments; takes action on drift early. | ✅ Engagement dashboard updated weekly; degrading attitude of one SME triggers a 1:1 from the sponsor. ❌ SME quietly disengages for 3 sprints; surface only when a critical requirement is missing. |
| EL-025 | Requirements traced backward to source stakeholder / document | §2.5; §9.6.2 | Medium | Each captured requirement is traced back to the originating stakeholder, session, or document. | ✅ "REQ-FR-014 — source: SME interview 2026-05-03; doc: GDPR Art. 17." ❌ No source captured; team can't decide who can change the requirement. |
| EL-026 | Pre-work delivered to participants in advance | §4.1.4 .5; §10.50.3 .1 | Medium | Agenda + pre-read sent in advance; for workshops, optional pre-workshop interviews conducted with participants. | ✅ Pre-read sent 48 h before; 80 % participants confirm they have read. ❌ Participants arrive without context; first 30 min lost re-introducing. |
| EL-027 | Confirmation is recorded and shared, not just verbal | §4.3 | Medium | Confirmation has a written trail (signed minutes, comment thread, e-mail acceptance) for traceability and to handle future disputes. | ✅ E-mail "I confirm" archived; or comment "approved" in the shared doc. ❌ Verbal "yes" in the meeting; SME later denies; nothing in writing. |
| EL-028 | Stakeholder collaboration approach explicitly chosen per group | §3.2.4 .2 | Medium | Frequency, location, tools, delivery method, and preferences are decided per stakeholder group; not "the same for everyone". | ✅ Plan: weekly 30-min sync with sponsor, async wiki for the dev team, monthly review for the steering committee. ❌ One weekly 60-min "all hands" meeting; nothing fits anyone. |
| EL-029 | Workshop has explicit roles (sponsor / facilitator / scribe / timekeeper / participants) | §10.50.3 .2 | Medium | Each role is assigned before the workshop, especially **facilitator ≠ scribe ≠ subject author**. | ✅ Facilitator from external team; scribe is a BA peer; sponsor opens and closes. ❌ The BA tries to facilitate + scribe + present material — neither is done well. |
| EL-030 | Workshop ground rules agreed at the start | §10.50.3 .3 | Medium | Ground rules (respect, everyone contributes, off-topic time-boxed, attack the issue not the person, decision rule) are stated and accepted at the opening. | ✅ Rules visible on the board; the facilitator references them when discussion derails. ❌ One vocal participant dominates and shoots down ideas; quiet SMEs disengage. |
| EL-031 | Privacy / confidentiality for elicitation data handled | §10.21 (Focus Groups) — confidentiality; §10.31 (Observation) — privacy | Medium | When observation, recording, focus group or interview involves personal data, consent, anonymisation, retention and disposal are decided in advance. | ✅ Consent form signed; recordings stored encrypted; deleted after analysis. ❌ Interview recording shared on a public drive; participants withdraw consent. |
| EL-032 | Prototype expectations managed | §10.36.4 .2 | Medium | When using prototypes, the BA explicitly communicates whether it is throw-away or evolutionary, and that visual polish does not imply functional completeness. | ✅ Stakeholders informed: "this is a paper prototype to test the flow, not the final UI". ❌ Stakeholders expect the polished Figma to ship as-is in two weeks. |
| EL-033 | Survey questions piloted before distribution | §10.45.3 .1 (Test the survey) | Low | A survey / questionnaire is pre-tested with a small group for clarity, length, and answerability before being distributed. | ✅ Pilot with 5 users; ambiguous questions rewritten. ❌ 200 surveys go out; 30 % response; one ambiguous question makes 40 % of answers unusable. |
| EL-034 | Lessons learned at the end of major sessions | §10.27; §4.5 | Low | After major sessions / phases, lessons learned are captured and fed back into the BA approach (what worked, what didn't, what to change next time). | ✅ 15-min retrospective at the end of each workshop; outcomes documented. ❌ Same mistakes (wrong room, missing SME, vague goal) repeated every session. |
| EL-035 | Tools and templates standardised across sessions | §9.6 | Low | Templates (interview guide, workshop agenda, item log, stakeholder map, glossary, RACI) and tools are consistent across sessions and reusable. | ✅ Shared template library; new BA can run a session in week 1. ❌ Each session reinvented from scratch; outputs hard to compare. |
